FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, N=9,475 with complete data, baseline mean age 9.9 years, enrolled 2016-2018, ongoing longitudinal study

FT_IC2.evidence: Smartphone age of acquisition (first smartphone ownership age) - qualifying exposure per B1; parents reported age child received first smartphone annually

FT_IC3.evidence: Cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression (Table 2); inferential tests with odds ratios, confidence intervals, and p-values; statistical significance defined as p<0.05

FT_IC4.evidence: Odds ratios (ORs) with 95% confidence intervals reported in Table 2 (e.g., Black: OR=3.01, 95% CI 2.50-3.64; Female: OR=1.38, 95% CI 1.19-1.60); p-values: p<0.001 and p<0.01

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis on ABCD data; new analysis examining sociodemographic predictors of early smartphone ownership

FT_IC6.evidence: Original publication, DOI 10.1542/peds.2025-073891, Pediatrics 2026, no evidence of preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: All three FT-EC7 conditions satisfied: (a) smartphone ownership age is DEPENDENT variable in ALL analyses - no SM→outcome analysis present; (b) all predictors (sex, race/ethnicity, household income, household structure, parental education) are in closed demographic-only set; (c) analysis is cross-sectional (baseline/baseline); cross-sectional carve-out applies per B15 so FT-EC7=TRUE
notes: Paper reports only cross-sectional baseline associations; no longitudinal/prospective analyses for SM prediction. All predictors are core demographics. Meets FT-EC7 criteria exactly.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD SM Study (Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Social Media Study), n=5,639, ages 10-13 years, US adolescents, cross-sectional data

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media qualifying variables: (1) SM hours per day (time on SM - continuous), (2) platform-specific use (TikTok, Instagram, Snapchat reported as most-used platforms), (3) SM accounts (public account, secret account reported as separate variables) - all meet B1 criteria for qualifying SM exposure

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression models (linear regression with unstandardized coefficients β and 95% CIs) examining associations between SM variables and SMA scale; statistical tests with confidence intervals and p-values reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression coefficients (unstandardized β) with 95% confidence intervals reported: TikTok (b=1.52, 95% CI 1.12, 1.93), Instagram (b=0.92, 95% CI 0.51, 1.34), Snapchat (b=0.60, 95% CI 0.16, 1.03), secret account (b=2.27, 95% CI 1.65, 2.89), SM hours (b=0.66, 95% CI 0.61, 0.71)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis on ABCD SM Study data; new analysis of associations between SM usage patterns and SMA scores

FT_IC6.evidence: Original publication, DOI 10.1016/j.jaacop.2026.02.003, JAACAP Open 2026, no evidence of duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply: SM is the DEPENDENT variable (SMA scale is outcome), but the analysis is SM-as-IV → SMA outcome, not SM-as-DV with demographic predictors only. The outcome is social media addiction scale (derived from SM variables), making this a within-SM construct analysis, not a demographic→SM prediction model. Does not meet FT-EC7 conditions.
notes: Paper focuses on SM usage patterns (hours, platforms, accounts) predicting social media addiction scale. Cross-sectional design. Primary exposure is SM variables themselves, outcome is SMA derived from SM behaviors.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, Year 2 (2018-2020) and Year 4 (2020-2022) follow-up, 4,443 adolescents with complete exposure and outcome data, diverse sample, US-based longitudinal cohort

FT_IC2.evidence: Recreational screen time - qualifying smartphone/screen use variable: self-reported total hours per day of screen use (watching television, playing video games, texting, social media, video chat) categorized as low (0-4), medium (>4-8), or high (>8) hours/day per B1

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression (for continuous outcomes: blood pressure percentile, cholesterol levels) and Poisson regression (for binary outcomes: hypertensive-range BP, low HDL, diabetes) with inferential tests; statistical significance determined by p-values and confidence intervals

FT_IC4.evidence: Adjusted regression coefficients with 95% CIs reported for primary outcomes: high screen time (>8 h/day) diastolic BP percentile increase 4.25 points (95% CI 1.85-6.65); low step count ARR 2.35 (95% CI 1.88-2.94); high screen time ARR 1.88 (95% CI 1.42-2.56); low step count HDL -3.72 mg/dL (95% CI -6.52 to -2.92)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis on ABCD Study Years 2-4 data; novel longitudinal analysis of joint associations between screen time, physical activity, and cardiovascular risk factors

FT_IC6.evidence: Original publication, DOI 10.1186/s12889-026-26756-z, BMC Public Health 2026, no evidence of duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply: screen time is the INDEPENDENT variable predicting non-SM outcomes (cardiovascular disease risk factors: blood pressure, cholesterol, hemoglobin A1c). This is a screen time→CVD outcome analysis, opposite of the FT-EC7 pattern (demographics→SM). FT-EC7 requires SM as DV with demographic-only predictors; this analysis has screen time as IV with CVD outcomes as DV.
notes: Longitudinal prospective design (Year 2→Year 4); screen time measured at baseline (Year 2) predicting cardiovascular outcomes at follow-up (Year 4). Adjusted for demographic and lifestyle covariates. Strong dose-response associations between screen time/step count and multiple CVD risk measures.
